title: 04_controlled_input_frozen.jsx
descriptionMd:
## Mission: Let the User Actually Type

The settings form renders a display-name field pre-filled with the user's current name. The field looks perfectly normal — it has a value, it accepts focus, and the cursor appears when you click it. But every keystroke is silently discarded. The field stays frozen at whatever `initialName` was when the component mounted, and anything the user types disappears before it can be submitted.

The submit handler reads `name` from state and POSTs it to `/api/settings`. The state update machinery is wired up correctly — `setName` exists and is called nowhere. That last detail is the diagnosis: a React controlled input with a `value` prop but no `onChange` handler is read-only by design. React owns the value, nothing tells React to update it, so every keystroke is a no-op.

Add the missing `onChange` handler so keystrokes reach the state and the field behaves as a standard editable input.
traceback:
Warning: You provided a `value` prop to a form field without an `onChange` handler. This will render a read-only field. If the field should be mutable use `defaultValue`. Otherwise, set either `onChange` or `readOnly`.
    at input
    at label
    at form
    at SettingsForm

FAIL  settings-form.test.jsx > user can edit their display name and save it

  TestingLibraryElementError: Unable to type into element
  The element has a controlled value prop but no onChange handler.
  User attempted: type "Alice Chen"
  Field rendered:  "" (unchanged — keystrokes were discarded)
correctOutput:
PASS  settings-form.test.jsx > user can edit their display name and save it

  console: POST /api/settings  { name: 'Alice Chen' }

options.label: Add an onChange handler that calls setName with the input's current value
options.resultLog:
PASS  settings-form.test.jsx > user can edit their display name and save it

  console: POST /api/settings  { name: 'Alice Chen' }
options.rationale: A controlled input is a two-way binding: React renders the value React holds in state, and `onChange` closes the loop by writing user input back into state. With `onChange={e => setName(e.target.value)}`, every keystroke fires a state update, React re-renders with the new value, and the field displays what the user typed. The submit handler was already reading `name` from state and posting it — adding the handler is the only change needed.

options.label: Replace value={name} with defaultValue={initialName} to make the field uncontrolled
options.resultLog:
FAIL  settings-form.test.jsx > submits the name the user typed, not the initial name

  AssertionError: POST body was { name: 'Jordan Lee' } (the initial prop)
  Expected:        { name: 'Alice Chen' } (what the user typed)

  Note: The field now accepts input, but handleSubmit still reads `name` from
  useState(initialName), which never updates — the DOM and the state are now out of sync.
options.rationale: Switching to `defaultValue` makes the field editable by surrendering React's control of it, but the submit handler still reads `name` from state — and `useState(initialName)` never updates because nothing calls `setName`. Whatever the user types lives only in the DOM; the POST body always contains the initial prop value. You've made the field look editable while breaking the submission. An uncontrolled input also can't be easily reset to a server-confirmed value after save, which controlled inputs handle naturally.

options.label: Add readOnly to the input to silence the React warning without changing the behavior
options.resultLog:
FAIL  settings-form.test.jsx > user can edit their display name and save it

  TestingLibraryElementError: Unable to type into element
  The element is read-only.
  User attempted: type "Alice Chen"
  Field rendered:  "" (unchanged — readOnly enforced)
options.rationale: `readOnly` is semantically correct when the intent is a display-only field, but the intent here is the opposite — the user is supposed to be able to edit their name. This option silences the console warning by making the read-only behavior *intentional* rather than accidental, but the test still fails because the field genuinely cannot be typed into. Suppressing a warning by entrenching the wrong behavior is worse than the original state: now there is no signal that the input is broken.

hints: React controlled inputs have a two-way contract: React sets the displayed value via `value`, and user input flows back to React via `onChange`. If `onChange` is missing, React holds the value and user keystrokes are discarded. What is needed to close the loop?

Line 21: `<input type="text" value={name} />` — the `value` prop binds the field to React state, but there is no `onChange` prop to write keystrokes back into state via `setName`. Adding `onChange={e => setName(e.target.value)}` completes the binding.
explanationMd:
### Why the bug occurs
In React, a form input with a `value` prop is *controlled* — React owns its displayed value. On every render React reads `name` from state and sets the field's value to match. When the user types, the browser fires an `input` event; React intercepts it, sees there is no `onChange` handler to update state, leaves `name` unchanged, and re-renders the field with the same value. The keystroke was processed and immediately undone. The console warning — "will render a read-only field" — is React making this explicit, but the field looks normal to users, so the bug ships.

### Why the fix is correct
`onChange={e => setName(e.target.value)}` closes the data-flow loop. Each keystroke fires the handler, `setName` updates state, React re-renders with the new value, and the field reflects what was typed. The submit handler was already reading `name` from state and posting it, so no other change is needed. The controlled pattern is preferred over uncontrolled (`defaultValue`) for forms because it keeps the source of truth in React state — making it easy to validate, reset, or derive computed values from the field without reaching into the DOM.
recruiterReview: Correct fix and the right frame: the issue is a broken two-way binding, not a missing feature. What stands out is the reasoning on the `readOnly` distractor — recognizing that suppressing a warning by entrenching the wrong behavior leaves the field broken while hiding the signal that it's broken is exactly the production instinct I want. The `defaultValue` trap is the other common stumble: it makes the field editable while silently decoupling the DOM from state, so the submit handler keeps posting the initial value. Senior reviewers watch for state/DOM divergence because it breeds subtle form submission bugs that only appear with pre-filled data. Approved. ✅

tutorial.bodyMd:
## Controlled vs. Uncontrolled Inputs in React

React offers two models for form inputs. An *uncontrolled* input behaves like a plain HTML input: the DOM holds its value, and you read it via a ref or from the form element on submit. A *controlled* input has a `value` prop driven by React state: React renders the value, and an `onChange` handler writes user input back into state on every keystroke.

### The two-way contract
The controlled pattern is a loop with two required halves:
1. `value={name}` — React renders the state value into the field on every render.
2. `onChange={e => setName(e.target.value)}` — user keystrokes update state, triggering a re-render.

If either half is missing the loop is open. Without `onChange`, React renders the state value, the user types, the browser fires an input event, React finds no handler, leaves the state unchanged, and re-renders the field with the same value — silently discarding every keystroke. The field looks interactive (cursor, focus, selection all work) but no text ever sticks.

### Why React warns but doesn't throw
React emits a console warning — "You provided a `value` prop without an `onChange` handler" — but does not throw an error, because a read-only field is sometimes intentional. If you want a controlled read-only display, add `readOnly` to make the intent explicit and silence the warning. If you want an editable field, add `onChange`. The warning exists precisely to tell you which case you are in when you forgot to decide.

### Uncontrolled inputs: when to use defaultValue
Switching to `defaultValue` makes the field uncontrolled: the DOM owns the value, React sets it once on mount and then steps back. This is appropriate for forms where you do not need to inspect or react to the field's contents during typing — simple submit-on-blur or native form submission. The trade-off is that you cannot easily reset the field programmatically, derive other state from it on each keystroke, or validate as-you-type. Any code that reads from React state after submit will get the initial value, not what the user typed.

### The controlled pattern in practice
For forms that need live validation, conditional UI based on input, or programmatic reset (e.g. after a successful save), controlled inputs are the right choice. The canonical shape is:
```jsx
const [value, setValue] = useState(initialValue);
// ...
<input value={value} onChange={e => setValue(e.target.value)} />
```
The onChange handler can be enriched: validate inline (`setValue(sanitize(e.target.value))`), cap length, transform to uppercase, or update multiple fields from one handler — always by calling the setter with the new desired state value.

### Common variations of this bug
- `value` set to a prop that never changes (a string constant, not state) — the field is permanently frozen at that string, no `onChange` helps because the prop never updates.
- `onChange` present but reading from the wrong state variable — the field appears to lag one keystroke behind because it renders the old state while the new state drives a different element.
- `defaultValue` used initially, then a code change adds `value=` without adding `onChange` — the field worked before the refactor, now it's frozen.
- `value={someObject.field}` where the object is mutated directly instead of through `setState` — the mutation changes the object but doesn't trigger a re-render, so the field shows stale content.
- A textarea or select with the same issue — both accept `value`/`onChange` in React and exhibit identical frozen behavior if `onChange` is absent.
